subject: Re: contrast: AREtomo vs WARP
body:
Hi all,

Just wanted to add a couple of comments to this. For Aretomo
reconstruction, if you run it without -Patch and -TiltCor then it should be
essentially identical to Warp's output. I'm assuming the XF files don't
change with the application of patches, although if that's not the case it
would be good to know. Also, I believe Warp does apply CTF correction to
the tomograms, or at least that's my interpretation of the methods section
in the paper (emphasis mine):

> Warp takes the* local defocus and sample distortion, as well as
> magnification anisotropy into account when reconstructing full or partial
> tomographic volumes*. For a partial reconstruction at any position in the
> volume, the original 2D images are sampled at the following positions:
>
> where *R**Euler* is the rotation matrix for 3 Euler angles following the
> Xmipp convention55
> <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR55>, *α* is the
> stage tilt angle, *ψ* is the in-plane angle of the tilt axis, *p* is the
> particle position within the tomographic volume, and *o* is the in-plane
> offset of the tilt axis. The coordinates are centered within the volume and
> images. The CTF for each 2D image is calculated using a defocus of:
>
> where *z**Tilt* is the average defocus estimated for the tilt image, *n*
> *Tilt* is the sample plane normal, *n**Tilt*,*z* is the z component of
> the normal and • denotes the scalar product between two vectors. *The
> reconstruction is performed in Fourier space using a gridding algorithm23
> <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR23>, with the data
> weighted by the respective CTF, and the dose- and tilt-dependent heuristic
> from RELION49 <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0580-y#ref-CR49>,
> but without the final deconvolution step (that is the weights are inserted
> as |CTF|, not as CTF2).* To obtain a full tomogram, Warp reconstructs a
> uniform grid of small, cubical volumes with an overlap of 50%, and inserts
> the central 50% into the overall volume to account for artifacts associated
> with Fourier space reconstruction at the borders of the local volumes. This
> ensures the corrections can be applied with local precision and remain
> reasonably continuous between adjacent sub-volumes.
>
Cheers,

Mat


On Thu, 23 Nov 2023 at 16:08, Ricardo Righetto <ricardorighetto@gmail.com>
wrote:

> One thing is yes, you should make sure that tilt series alignments are
> exactly the same between AreTomo and Warp for a fair comparison. And as
> Pablo said, if you do local patch corrections in AreTomo these will not be
> applied in Warp.
>
> Furthermore, Warp does not apply CTF correction to full tomograms as far
> as I know, so this would be the same (no CTF) in both reconstructions.
>
> My guess (and it's just a guess) is that each program filters the
> reconstructions in different ways, i.e. they apply different weighting
> schemes during backprojection.
>
> --
> Ricardo Diogo Righetto
>
>
> Em qui., 23 de nov. de 2023 às 16:03, Lorenzo Gaifas <
> lorenzo.gaifas@gmail.com> escreveu:
>
>> Hi all,
>>
>> Forwarding my own message to the list cause I just noticed I only replied
>> directly by mistake.
>>
>> Best,
>> Lorenzo
>>
>> ---------- Forwarded message ---------
>> From: Lorenzo Gaifas <lorenzo.gaifas@gmail.com>
>> Date: Thu, Nov 23, 2023 at 3:08 PM
>> Subject: Re: contrast: AREtomo vs WARP
>> To: mrJ <bluufive@gmail.com>
>>
>>
>> Hi Mrj,
>>
>> In my experience, this is typical (no filtering in warp), though it might
>> also be caused by the alignments being off (many steps along the way could
>> go wrong, tilt series ordering, angles, and so on).
>>
>> Do the features in the deconvolved version in warp look more visible at
>> least?
>>
>> For a better comparison, you can also just apply a simple gaussian blur
>> to the warp reconstruction to get rid of the high res noise.
>>
>> Best,
>> Lorenzo
>>
>> On Thu, Nov 23, 2023 at 1:36 PM mrJ <bluufive@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>>> dear community,
>>>
>>> I have included AREtomo as part of my WARP pipeline in order to get .xf
>>> file for alignment.  The AREtomo tomograms look quite nice, however when I
>>> use the .xf file to generate the WARP tomogram I notice the contrast is
>>> substantially worse.  See image below.  The tomogram looks otherwise
>>> reasonable, it just has really bad contrast.  For AREtomo I was also using
>>> WBP (which I think WARP does).
>>>
>>> Is this to be expected? is it because WARP has corrected CTF and
>>> therefore less contrast?
>>>
>>> thanks!
>>>
>>>
>>>
>>> [image: Screenshot 2023-11-23 at 1.35.02 PM.png]
>>>
